title: Ten years later, Blaze alumni are representing in MLB
description: It has been 10 years since the loss of the Bakersfield Blaze, the last minor-league baseball team to play in Sam Lynn Ballpark with a Major League Baseball affiliation. The Blaze was in Bakersfield from 1995 to 2016 and had affiliation with the Giants, Rays, Rangers, Reds and lastly, the Mariners. While the franchise is no longer around, there are present-day baseball connections — MLB ...
fullText:
It has been 10 years since the loss of the Bakersfield Blaze, the last minor-league baseball team to play in Sam Lynn Ballpark with a Major League Baseball affiliation.

The Blaze was in Bakersfield from 1995 to 2016 and had affiliation with the Giants, Rays, Rangers, Reds and lastly, the Mariners. While the franchise is no longer around, there are present-day baseball connections — MLB connections — to note.

Tim Wheeler is a local sports statistician who works at numerous Bakersfield College and Cal State Bakersfield sporting events and had been working baseball games at Sam Lynn Ballpark, including the Blaze's entire run (1,500-plus games) before retiring from the current tenant, the independent Train Robbers, in 2025. He sent in an interesting list he recently researched.

He tracked down all former Bakersfield Blaze players who are currently playing professionally, either in MLB or in the minors and have MLB experience.

One player, New York Yankees pitcher Ryan Yarbrough, just faced the Los Angeles Dodgers on Sunday. L.A. was in New York for the first series after last week’s MLB All-Star Game.

Yarbrough, who pitched for the Blaze in 2015, is one of six current MLB players with Blaze ties and one of five pitchers. The others are the Colorado Rockies’ Michael Lorenzen (Blaze, 2013), San Diego Padres’ Wandy Peralta (2014), Washington Nationals’ Zack Littrell (2016) and current injured Dodgers relief pitcher Edwin Diaz (2015). The lone non-pitcher is Baltimore Orioles outfielder Tyler O’Neill (2015).

Wheeler also found three more Blaze players — all pitchers — who are currently in the minors, have MLB experience, but are not currently on the parent club’s 40-man roster. They are Ben Lively, Columbus (Cleveland), Dan Altavilla, Omaha (Kansas City), and Anthony Misiewicz, Louisville (Cincinnati). The three were on the Blaze in 2014, 2015 and 2016, respectively.

The Bakersfield Train Robbers baseball team swept a two-game weekend series at Sam Lynn Ballpark against the Austin Weirdos in Pecos League play. Bakersfield won 8-3 on Friday and then 17-9 on Saturday.

In Friday’s game, the Weirdos scored three runs in the second inning for its only lead in the game. The Train Robbers countered and took the lead for good in the fourth inning. Brock Slikker reached base on a fielder’s choice, enabling one run to score. Zach Beatty reached first on an error, getting another run across. When Beatty stole second, the catcher’s throw was errant and yet another runner scored for a 4-3 lead. Macs Carrillo then singled to drive in Beatty. In the fifth inning, Beaty drove in two runs with a single and Maxim Fullerton added another RBI single in the sixth.

Slikker, who was the starting pitcher for Bakersfield, went 5 1/3 innings and struck out six to even his season record to 3-3.

The Train Robbers offense was on full display on Saturday, namely Fullerton and Joe Riddle. Fullerton drove in seven runs and Riddle added four more against Austin.

It was 6-0 after the first inning as Riddle had an RBI single, Cade Fujii had a RBI single and a few batters later, Fullerton hit a grand slam. Austin got a few runs back in the third, but Riddle had a two-run single in the fourth inning. Austin did get the score to within 10-7 with a three-run homer by Cono Casale in the sixth, but Fullerton had a pair of RBI doubles in consecutive innings and Riddle had a sacrifice fly. Fullerton’s final run-scoring play was a fielder’s choice in the eighth.

While starter Levi Tucker and Brenden Poindexter each got roughed up a bit early, Lucas Belcher (3-0) got the win with two innings of scoreless relief.

The Train Robbers, who are 20-7 at home this season and 26-15-1 overall, started a three-game home series with Dublin on Monday. The teams play at 7:45 p.m. on Tuesday and Wednesday. These games will close out Bakersfield’s regular-season home schedule. The team will be on the road for games this Friday at Dublin and Saturday at Martinez, both starting at 6 p.m. Then they will begin 2 p.m. Sunday at Dublin. The playoffs start Monday.

The start of the regular season is less than a month away, but the CSUB women’s soccer team will be playing a Blue vs. White scrimmage game at 7 p.m. Saturday at the CSUB soccer field.

It will be the first opportunity to see the 2026 team, which is now directed by interim head coach Martin Navarro Jr. He is no stranger to the program. This will be his fifth year as Navarro has been with the team since 2022 when he began his coaching career as a graduate assistant coach. He was promoted in summer 2023 as an assistant coach and goalkeeper coach and became associate head coach in January. He was named interim head coach in March.

The Roadrunners open their 2026 season on the road, 6 p.m. Aug. 13 against the University of San Francisco. CSUB’s home opener is 11 a.m. Aug. 23 against Idaho State.

title: Houston Rockets jersey history No. 22 - Eric McWilliams 1972-73)
description: During his time suiting up for the Rockets, McWilliams wore only jersey No. 22 and put up 2.0 points and 1.4 rebounds per game.
fullText:
The Houston Rockets have had players donning a total of 52 different jersey numbers (and have one not part of any numerical series for Houston assistant coach and general manager Carroll Dawson) since their founding at the start of the 1967-68 season, worn by just under 500 players in the course of Rockets history.

To honor all of the players who wore those numbers over the decades, Rockets Wire is covering the entire history of jersey numbers and the players who wore them since the founding of the team all those years ago right up to the present day.

With seven of those jerseys now retired to honor some of the greatest Rockets of all time to wear those jerseys, there is a lot of history to cover.

And for today's article, we will continue with the second of eight who wore the No. 22, forward alum Eric McWilliams. After ending his college career at Cal State Long Beach, McWilliams was picked up with the 37th overall selection of the 1972 NBA Draft by the Houston Rockets.

The Denver, Colorado native played the sole season of his pro career with Houston, leaving the league as a player afterward.

title: Thousands welcome home Argentina squad, without Messi, despite World Cup loss
description: Thousands of fans braved the cold and rain Monday to give a rapturous welcome to the Argentine football squad who landed back home without star Lionel Messi, after their World Cup final loss to Spain."Today it's hard to appreciate what we achieved, but this squad reached two consecutive World Cup finals."
fullText:
Thousands of fans braved the cold and rain Monday to give a rapturous welcome to the Argentine football squad who landed back home without star Lionel Messi, after their World Cup final loss to Spain.

It was not the homecoming Argentines had hoped for, four years after millions celebrated their team's tournament victory in 2022, but fresh scenes of joyful chaos filled the street as they danced, sang and set off fireworks to thank the players.

The Argentine Football Association (AFA) said earlier that some squad members would not be on the flight, and iconic team captain Messi was not seen descending from the aircraft in images broadcast on local television.

Coach Lionel Scaloni and the rest of the team were welcomed with a red carpet outside the aircraft and a military band.

They then boarded a bus, waving to frenzied fans along the way.

Despite falling short of a second straight championship, "Argentines will celebrate anyway," said housewife Marga Ledezma. The 36-year-old thanked Messi for "all the years he made us happy," urging him not to quit playing for Argentina, but admitting this would be difficult as he was 39.

Argentina lost 1-0 to Spain in a game that has been criticized in international media for a plethora of fouls and displays of poor sportsmanship from the Albiceleste.

"I think they criticize us because we know what it's like to suffer, that we get up, we fall down again, and we keep going," said Ledezma.

Many fans highlighted that the real victory for Argentina was the 2-1 semi-final win over England, played against the backdrop of a lingering dispute between the countries over the sovereignty of the Falkland Islands.

Some carried flags declaring "The Falklands are Argentine." FIFA is investigating the Argentine team after players held up a banner bearing the phrase after their win against England which sparked a backlash from London.

"This team united us all, and we should be grateful for that. We will always be there for them, rain or shine. I would say thank you to Messi and that he gave it his all," said Leonardo Barrientos, 36, who works in a textile workshop.

Maria Ortiz, 28, came to welcome the team with her two-year-old, and said Spain "deserved the win" but she was "happy for what the team gave us."

Messi wrote on Instagram on Monday that "the pain is immense and it's going to take a long time for this wound to heal."

"I'll also hold on to all the good memories," he wrote alongside a photo of himself. "Today it's hard to appreciate what we achieved, but this squad reached two consecutive World Cup finals."

With the tournament over, President Javier Milei announced that a future date agreed with the team would be set as a public holiday to celebrate their performance in the World Cup. No further details have been announced.

While crestfallen after the loss, thousands of Argentines headed to the traditional celebration point at the Obelisk in Buenos Aires where they partied for hours on Sunday evening.

Late at night, brief clashes broke out at the monument and police used water cannons, tear gas, and rubber bullets to disperse revellers. Fifteen people were detained, according to police sources.

title: Jessica Fox embraced 'cowgirl confidence' with ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships win
description: Olympic champion and Australian Jessica Fox celebrated her ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships gold medal win in OKC by wearing cowboy boots on the podium.
fullText:
Jessica Fox wore her usual medal ceremony outfit: Australia’s bright yellow jacket, green warmup pants and a Red Bull cap.

But standing atop the first-place podium at Riversport OKC, the three-time Olympic canoe slalom champion added one distinctly local touch.

Fox is just one of over 300 international athletes competing in this week's ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships, giving paddlers a taste of Oklahoma City and its culture as they hope to return for the 2028 Summer Olympics.

“If I won a medal, I would wear the boots on the podium because we’re in Oklahoma, right?” Jessica Fox said. “Gotta embrace the cowgirl style and that cowgirl confidence.”

More: What's the whitewater course like at Riversport OKC? We hopped on the rapids to find out

Less than a year after undergoing kidney surgery, the most decorated canoe slalom paddler in history added another gold medal Monday, winning the kayak cross individual time trial.

The Fox family is canoe slalom royalty. Jessica's younger sister, Noemie, won Olympic gold in kayak cross at the 2024 Paris Games, and both of their parents were international medalists in the sport.

The Fox sisters have embraced Oklahoma City just as much as the city has embraced them. Australians living in Oklahoma have invited them out for coffee, helping make their stay feel more like home.

“We interacted with a lot of local people who were just so lovely, and you can really feel that hospitality and that Oklahoma standard,” Jessica Fox said.

Local fans filled the shaded viewing areas Monday morning, turning out to watch the 4-hour opening event despite the near 100-degree Oklahoma heat.

“I think it's a really great venue to host a major event, and it's really cool to see so many locals supporting that,” Noemie Fox said.

The Fox sisters have a bucket list of places they still want to visit around Oklahoma City. This trip, they crossed the Oklahoma City National Memorial & Museum off that list.

Noemie also wants to buy another pair of cowboy boots, this time with tassels, and go line dancing again. She hopes to do just that in two years, returning for the Olympic stage.

“I'm excited to come back, and I have a whole list of things that we didn't really get to do this time round,” Noemie Fox said. “I think that's the best way to leave a city with a lot of things left to do, and yeah I want to come back.”

More: Why ICF Canoe Slalom World Championships will be nice Olympic tune-up for OKC

title: Cincinnati Reds: Burns 'thankful' to sign long-term contract with the Reds
description: DENVER — While the 2026 season has been a disappointing one for the Cincinnati Reds, 23-year-old starting pitcher Chase Burns looks around the clubhouse and sees a lot of talent, people that he trusts and an organization that he has confidence in heading into the future. On Saturday, July 18, Burns officially signed a seven-year, $105 million extension with the Reds. “It doesn’t feel real ...
fullText:
DENVER — While the 2026 season has been a disappointing one for the Cincinnati Reds, 23-year-old starting pitcher Chase Burns looks around the clubhouse and sees a lot of talent, people that he trusts and an organization that he has confidence in heading into the future.

On Saturday, July 18, Burns officially signed a seven-year, $105 million extension with the Reds.

“It doesn’t feel real yet,” Burns said. “Next year or the year after, it’ll start to hit me. I’m not really worried about all of that. I don’t play the game for money. I play the game for the opportunity. I just like to keep it mellow. It was what it is. It’s a great opportunity. The biggest thing is staying humble and staying hungry.”

The structure of Burns’ contract goes like this: $2.5 million in 2027, $8 million in 2028, $12.5 in 2029, $15 million in 2030, $17 million in 2031, $23 million in 2032 and $25 million in 2033. While the contract officially begins next season, Burns did receive a $2 million signing bonus.

“The contract has to start next year because it’s after the 21st day of the season,” President of Baseball Operations Nick Krall said. “But we gave him a signing bonus up front. And then we front-loaded a little bit of it. We tried to smooth it out a little bit from where the arbitration and zero-to-three process would have taken him.”

Extension talks really picked up in the middle of June, around the Reds’ series against the Yankees. With the deal, the Reds gain two additional years of team control and push Burns’ free agency back to after the 2033 season.

“Chase is an exciting young pitcher, an All-Star this year, and the sky is the limit for him,” Krall said.

It’s not very common for young pitchers to sign long-term extensions. There’s risk involved due to injuries, but the Reds did go through a process of taking out insurance on this extension with Burns to help protect them in case there’s an injury.

One of the only comparable contract extensions in baseball for a young pitcher like Burns, who hasn’t hit arbitration yet, is the extension that Hunter Greene signed with the Reds in 2023.

“(Burns’ deal) is very exciting,” Greene said. “A lot of peace of mind for him. For him to be able to get that is a testament to his work ethic and the position he has been able to put himself in. It’ll also give him a little more motivation to keep getting better.”

Sal Stewart remains an obvious candidate for the Reds to extend. Krall said that there aren’t contract extension talks with other players in process right now.

The Reds are the only team in all of baseball with multiple position players (Elly De La Cruz and Sal Stewart) as well as multiple pitchers (Greene, Burns and Andrew Abbott) who are 27 years old or younger and have been All-Stars over the last three seasons.

While they’re having a frustrating season, there’s still confidence in this young core.

“This organization put a lot of trust in me,” Burns said. “They drafted me two years ago. They put that confidence in me. They supported me. It’s family. There’s a reason why God put me in this opportunity. I’m thankful for it, and I hope to win a championship here.”

title: Dodgers Provide Update on Shohei Ohtani Pitching Timeline
description: Los Angeles Dodgers manager Dave Roberts provided an updated timeline for Shohei Ohtani's return to pitching.
fullText:
Los Angeles Dodgers manager Dave Roberts spoke about Shohei Ohtani’s hiatus from pitching on ESPN’s broadcast of LA’s game against the Philadelphia Phillies, and provided a general timeline for when the two-way star may return to the mound.

When asked about Ohtani’s status, Roberts said it will be “at least a week to 10 days” until the Dodgers think about having their ace pitch again.

Dave Roberts, on the ESPN broadcast, says it will be "at least a week to 10 days" until the Dodgers even entertain the idea of Shohei Ohtani pitching.

Ohtani played catch again today pregame, and Roberts hopes he will get off the mound later this week. pic.twitter.com/fvbrgJOUDY

— Dodgers Nation (@DodgersNation) July 21, 2026

Ohtani has dealt with a lingering issue in his knee since June, and it has impacted his playing time for both the Dodgers and the NL All-Stars. Ohtani missed one of his scheduled starts before the All-Star break, then missed a game entirely while dealing with the injury.

Additionally, the reigning MVP missed the All-Star game while he underwent a procedure on his knee.

Prior to Sunday’s doubleheader against the New York Yankees, Roberts revealed Ohtani would not pitch in his next schedule start, which would have been against the Phillies on Wednesday in the series finale.

“Shohei’s not going to start on Wednesday,” Roberts said. “His knee, we just want to continue to give it the best chance to get into a good spot. As he said, as we feel, it’s the pitching and the torque of landing on that leg that gets it upset. I think that the hitting doesn’t do that, so we’re trying to give it as much time as possible.”

While they still have plenty of talent to fill in for Ohtani, it will be difficult to replicate the results MLB’s only two-way player has produced so far this season.

Ohtani has only made 14 starts on the season, but has made the most of each of his appearances on the mound in 2026. He has a 1.79 ERA through his starts, which span 85.2 innings pitched. He also has 95 strikeouts this season.

Justin Wrobleski will stand in for Ohtani in the final game of the series, but that still leaves the issue of who will replace the right-hander while he remains sidelined.

Two-Time Cy Young winner Blake Snell is due back from his stint on the injured list shortly, having already begun his rehab assignment with Triple-A Oklahoma City. Regardless of Snell’s status, the Dodgers will hope Ohtani can get over the hump with his knee issue and continue his two-way duties.

title: How young is the average age of Texas football’s roster?
description: One of the points raised about Texas football’s roster throughout the current offseason has been the average age of the program’s roster heading into the 2026 college football season. In fact, as noted by multiple outlets, Texas has the fourth-youngest roster across the entire sport at an average roster age of 19.86 years old. In […]
fullText:
One of the points raised about Texas football’s roster throughout the current offseason has been the average age of the program’s roster heading into the 2026 college football season. In fact, as noted by multiple outlets, Texas has the fourth-youngest roster across the entire sport at an average roster age of 19.86 years old. In other words, roughly the age of a second-year sophomore.

Most of the discussion around average roster age goes back to a recent RotoWire report.

Here’s an excerpt from how RotoWire broke it down at the time:

“Across all 138 FBS programs, the average roster checks in at just over 20.3 years old — but the gap between the oldest and youngest teams is wider than a full calendar year, and it says a great deal about how each program is built. At the top sits UTSA, whose roster averages 21.02 years old — the oldest in the country. At the other end … the youngest roster in the sport at 19.81. Neither is an accident. In the transfer-portal era, veteran-heavy rosters and blue-chip youth movements are two different blueprints for winning — and the numbers below show exactly who is leaning which way.”

Clearly, it’s not even a big deal by most measures of the game.

At least those that matter most—wins and losses.

Georgia football, for example, has the game’s current youngest average age roster.

Georgia has also played in consecutive College Football Playoff quarterfinals.

title: Jorge Jesus confirms talks with CBF and digs at Ancelotti call-up
description: After being eliminated from the FIFA 2026 World Cup in the round of 16, with a 1-0 defeat to Spain, the Portugal national team announced the hiring of coach Jorge Jesus on July 10.The former Flamengo ...
fullText:
After being eliminated from the FIFA 2026 World Cup in the round of 16, with a 1-0 defeat to Spain, the Portugal national team announced the hiring of coach Jorge Jesus on July 10.

The former Flamengo manager arrived to take over the team in place of the dismissed Roberto Martínez.

This Monday (20), Jorge Jesus confirmed that he held talks last year to take charge of the Brazilian national team, after the 4-1 defeat to Argentina that led to Dorival Júnior’s departure.

"I ended up not going to coach the Brazilian national team, but I had already been given indications to make Brazil’s preliminary squad list for the match that would be played in March after they had lost 4-1 to Argentina," JJ told Portugal’s Canal 11.

In fact, after the defeat to their South American rivals in the qualifiers, the national team’s next commitment came during the FIFA window in June 2025, when Carlo Ancelotti made his debut in charge of Brazil.

Jorge Jesus even took a swipe at the Italian coach’s squad selection for the 2026 World Cup.

According to the new Portugal coach, Pedro, whom he worked with at Flamengo, is the country’s best "striker" (center forward) and deserved a place among those called up.

"What would I have done? Brazilian players are all over the world. Besides that, there is a lot of quality in the Brazilian Championship... And would I have called up a different squad from what Ancelotti did? No, of course I would have with one or two players. I think that right now the best striker is Pedro, and that is a little biased because he was my player at Flamengo. One to three different players, but I agree with most of what he did. But the way of looking at the game and training is the difference."

This article was translated into English by Artificial Intelligence. You can read the original version in 🇧🇷 here.

title: Dodgers Make Massive Halvorsen Acquisition To Fix Struggling Bullpen
description: The Dodgers traded two former early draft picks for Seth Halvorsen, a high-velocity reliever recovering from injury, to add bullpen depth.
fullText:
The Dodgers finally pulled the trigger on their first deadline trade Monday, snatching right-handed reliever Seth Halvorsen from the Colorado Rockies. This deal plugs a gaping hole for more arms in the bullpen. The club sent back two minor-leaguers to land this high-velocity pitcher. It’s a low-profile move designed to build immediate depth for the playoff push.

Halvorsen brings that massive fastball to Los Angeles, a weapon hitters simply can’t ignore. That speed is exactly what the Dodgers need right now to lock down late-inning games. Fans should expect to see his name on the roster very soon.

Per reports, the Dodgers acquired Seth Halvorsen in exchange for two minor-leaguers. They’re shipping back Nick Frasso, a former top-ranked pitching prospect who has struggled with injuries over his career. The Dodgers are also sending back Landyn Vidourek, the organization’s 2025 third-round pick (a date that shows how young he is). Vidourek has slumped in his first full season with single-A Ontario this year.

Halvorsen, 26, will report to triple-A immediately. He had been on a rehab assignment recovering from shoulder inflammation. He’s a third-year big-leaguer with a 99 mph fastball. His career stats show he can handle major league hitters. Halvorsen has 13 career saves with a 4.31 ERA in 75 MLB appearances. His career ERA+ is 114, which proves he performs above average.

The Post’s Joel Sherman and Jon Heyman broke the details of this swap. The Dodgers acted quickly to secure a reliever with that kind of velocity. They gave up two former early draft picks to get the job done. Frasso and Vidourek were both highly regarded prospects at one time. The Dodgers clearly value immediate help over long-term potential here.

Halvorsen’s shoulder inflammation was a concern for Colorado. The Dodgers believe he is ready to contribute soon. They took a calculated risk on a pitcher with a 99 mph fastball. This trade shows the Dodgers are willing to spend draft capital for bullpen help. The move targets a specific need rather than a blockbuster name.

The Dodgers must add velocity to their bullpen right now. Halvorsen’s 99 mph fastball changes the equation for opposing hitters. A 114 ERA+ means he gets outs better than the average pitcher. The Dodgers need that kind of production in tight games. They can’t afford to lose leads because of weak relief pitching.

Trading Nick Frasso was the right call for Los Angeles. He has struggled with injuries over his career. The risk of keeping him is too high for a contender. Landyn Vidourek is also a smart piece to move. He has slumped in his first full season with single-A Ontario this year. The Dodgers are cleaning up their farm system for immediate help.

Halvorsen will report to triple-A to finish his rehab. This gives the Dodgers time to monitor his shoulder health. They won’t rush him into a high-leverage spot. The team must be patient with a pitcher recovering from shoulder inflammation. But once he is ready, his 99 mph fastball will be a weapon.

The Dodgers are building depth for the long playoff run. They need every arm they can get. Halvorsen fits that profile perfectly. He is a third-year big-leaguer with proven experience. The Dodgers will use him to shore up the back end of the bullpen. This trade shows they are serious about winning the World Series.

Keep an eye on Halvorsen’s progress through the minor leagues. He must clear his rehab assignment from shoulder inflammation. Once he returns, expect him to join the big league roster. The Dodgers will likely use him in low-pressure spots first. His 99 mph fastball will be the main attraction for fans.

The Dodgers must manage his workload carefully. A 4.31 ERA in 75 MLB appearances shows he has been effective. But the shoulder injury is the biggest variable here. If he stays healthy, he could be a key piece. The Dodgers will need all their relievers to stay healthy for October. This trade adds a crucial layer of security to their pitching staff.

title: The Ville dominates La Familia to force a winner-take-all Game 3 in Lexington
description: La Familia and The Ville will meet Wednesday night at Memorial Coliseum with a trip to the next round on the line.
fullText:
After escaping with a 72-68 victory in Game 1 at Memorial Coliseum, La Familia couldn’t replicate that performance Monday night, falling to The Ville in convincing fashion at Freedom Hall to set up a decisive Game 3.

La Familia led 15-12 after the first quarter, but The Ville completely took over from there. La Familia managed just seven points in the second quarter as The Ville exploded for 24, taking a 36-22 halftime lead.

The Ville continued to control the game after the break, outscoring La Familia 21-17 in the third quarter and never looking back.

Montrezl Harrell was the biggest difference, finishing with a dominant 35-point performance after scoring 18 in the first half. With Marcus Lee and Archie Goodwin unavailable, and Willie Cauley-Stein leaving early with a foot injury, La Familia had no answer for Harrell inside.

La Familia also struggled offensively, shooting just 20-of-59 (33.9%) from the field and 8-of-25 (32%) from 3-point range while being outrebounded 43-30.

Now, the rivalry heads back to Lexington for a winner-take-all Game 3 on Wednesday, July 22, at 6 p.m. ET inside Memorial Coliseum. If La Familia wants to advance, it will need to slow down Harrell, rebound better and shoot much more efficiently.

There was encouraging news, as sideline reporter Jen Hale shared that La Familia general manager Twany Beckham has six players on standby for Game 3.

title: Flathub's AI slop ban looks like it was the right call
description: When Flathub banned AI-coded app submissions last month, some critics warned the platform was denying the future by dismissing a new wave of
fullText:
When Flathub banned AI-coded app submissions last month, some critics warned the platform was denying the future by dismissing a new wave of “vibe-coded” software as out-and-out “slop”.

Well, new data suggests otherwise, as nearly three-quarters of the rejected apps are already dead, existing only a few months.

Linux developer Evangelos Paterakis, developer of Tuba, Turntable and others, did the digging, looking at 120 code repositories whose pull requests for inclusion on Flathub were rejected because of their heavy AI usage and given an “AI Slop1” tag.

Of those rejected, 88 are no longer under active development, many having deleted their source code entirely. Just 32 still show signs of life, but it makes for a 73% abandonment rate overall.

It nixes the idea users, so keen to benefit from a new wave of AI-coded wares, would rush off to use other formats and app stores since, it turns out, whatever might have been swept up in the ban didn’t appear to be anything anyone felt bothered about maintaining anyway.

The methodology used here was not rigorous or academic. Paterakis acknowledges this themselves in a blog post titled “Democratizing Abandonware“ (which is terrifically subtitled: “I’m not digging through this cemetery”).

Open-source has always been a graveyard of solo hobby projects – something AI has amplified

And, sure, abandonment/attrition isn’t unique to AI. Open source has always been a graveyard for solo hobby projects, irrespective of whether a chatbot helped to write them or not.

But the lower barrier and the lack of personal involvement certainly amplifies it; fickle is as ever it was.

I know; I’ve spent ~16 years writing about new software and distros that kick off with enthusiasm, get people excited, only to succumb to the cobwebs and crows once the existing part wanes.

But Paterakis’ findings are not apropos of nothing either.

This data justifies Flathub’s ban was: volunteer reviewers being stuck reading reams of code the submitter hadn’t, then wasting time arguing with a machine agent (or a meat-sack intermediary) confidently dispensing word salad rather than actioning the fixes.

Debate rages on what constitutes slopware – to me, 20 music players that look like increasingly garbled versions of Amberol drawn through through a game of telephone comes close – but a musing from Thom Holwerda at OSNews vibed with me.

Generating code that runs was never the hard part – it was making software that people want to use

His point is that if AI tools really do enable developers to focus more on quality and innovation – as the marketing and hype suggests – shouldn’t we be seeing some sign of it by now?

Sure, more apps are being made, but are they good apps or just vibe-coded simulacrum of things we already have, e.g., Pomodoro timers, note-taking apps, applets around APIs – things beginner coders were making before AI.

Because, to me, despite the boosterism, there’s little complexity or innovation to what’s being produced, and little thought given to why – no identifying unmet needs and designing a solution one can use AI to build. Where’s the vibe-coded Photoshop clone for Linux?

Generating code that runs was never the hard or the magical part, it was making software that answers a need and which people want to use.

Perhaps, like a return on AI investment, it’s a waiting game. The really compelling vibe-coded software is just around the corner, as soon as that next data center is online, in orbit, chained to a blackhole or wherever else the goalposts have been moved to.

title: FDE Interview Questions
description: Every category of question asked in Forward Deploy Engineer interviews at Palantir-style enterprise platform companies — behavioral, domain-modeling, technical, case-study, and executive-communication — with sample answers, frameworks, and a 2-week prep plan.
fullText:
July 17, 2026 · Forward Deploy Engineering · 17 min read

A Forward Deploy Engineer (FDE) interview loop does not look like a typical backend or full-stack loop. Companies that hire FDEs — Palantir, Anduril, Anthropic’s Applied AI / Solutions org, OpenAI’s Solutions team, Scale AI, and the growing wave of vertical AI startups (Harvey, Sierra, Decagon, Hippocratic, and others) — are not just testing whether you can write correct code. They are testing whether you can:

So the loop blends algorithmic screening (lighter than a typical SWE loop), live domain-modeling exercises (heavier than almost any other engineering interview), case studies and take-homes (closer to a consulting interview), and behavioral rounds that probe judgment under ambiguity far more than they probe conflict resolution platitudes.

If you prepare for this loop the way you’d prepare for a generic FAANG loop — grinding LeetCode and rehearsing a single STAR story — you will be underprepared for at least half of it. This tutorial covers all of it.

Loops vary by company, but the shape is consistent enough to prepare against:

Not every company runs all of these, and some compress two into one round. But if you can perform in each of these seven modes, you are prepared for essentially any FDE loop.

These come early (recruiter screen, first onsite round) and eliminate candidates who romanticize the role without understanding its costs.

Weak answer pattern to avoid: talking exclusively about the mission or the company’s prestige without any specific evidence you’ve operated the way an FDE operates (fast context absorption, comfort with mess, shipping under ambiguity).

FDE behavioral rounds probe judgment under ambiguity and stakeholder conflict far more than generic “tell me about a conflict with a coworker” questions. Use the STAR format (Situation, Task, Action, Result), but weight your answer toward decisions made under incomplete information.

A strong STAR answer, worked example (Q6):

Situation: Three weeks into an engagement, the customer’s ops VP wanted a live demo for their CEO on a Friday, but our data pipeline only had partial coverage — two of five warehouses were feeding clean data, three were still being validated. Task: Decide whether to demo with partial data (risking a “this doesn’t work” impression) or delay (risking loss of executive sponsorship momentum). Action: I scoped a demo that explicitly showed the two working warehouses live, and presented the other three as a named roadmap item with dates, rather than hiding the gap or overpromising completeness. I looped in the VP the day before so there were no surprises in the room. Result: The CEO signed off on continued investment specifically because the demo was honest about scope — the VP later told me the “half is real, half is roadmap” framing was more convincing than a fully-simulated demo would have been. What I’d do differently: I’d have flagged the data gap to the VP a week earlier instead of three days out, giving more room to adjust the plan.

Notice the shape: a real trade-off, a specific decision, a quantifiable or verifiable result, and an honest retrospective correction. Panels are trained to probe vague answers (“we communicated better” / “I learned to listen more”) with a follow-up “what specifically did you say?” — have the specifics ready.

This is the round that differentiates FDE loops from almost every other engineering interview. You will typically be given a short, informal business scenario — often delivered verbally, like a stakeholder interview — and asked to build a domain model live, usually on a whiteboard or shared doc, while the interviewer plays the role of an evasive or imprecise stakeholder.

How to approach it (the method to narrate out loud):

What gets you eliminated in this round:

These rounds test the “can you actually build it” half of the role — usually data plumbing, integration design, and light system design rather than deep algorithmic questions.

Sample SQL-style question, worked:

“Find all shipments more than 24 hours late that haven’t been flagged.”

Talk through the edge case out loud: what if actual_arrival is set but still later than expected_arrival by more than 24 hours — is that “late” too, or only shipments still in flight? State the assumption you’re making and why (e.g., “I’m treating already-arrived-late shipments as a separate reporting case, not this alert, because this query is meant to drive an operator’s action queue for shipments still in flight”).

Take-homes simulate a compressed engagement: you get a dataset (or a written scenario) and a business problem, and you’re asked to produce an artifact — a data model, a short technical plan, sometimes a working prototype.

How these are actually graded (this is the part most candidates get wrong): graders are not primarily scoring code quality or completeness. They’re scoring:

A take-home that ships a smaller, well-reasoned, clearly-scoped MVP with a one-paragraph “what I’d ask a real stakeholder next” section consistently beats a take-home that ships more code with no reasoning attached.

Some loops include a dedicated round where you present a plan or a result to a panel playing the role of executives — often deliberately impatient, interrupting, or skeptical.

The framework to use: BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front).

Start with the conclusion, then the supporting evidence, then the plan — never build up to the point. A weak opening: “So we started by looking at the data, and there were some interesting patterns, and eventually we found…” A strong opening: “The pilot cut average dispatch time by 22%. Here’s how, and here’s what it takes to scale it to all three regions.”

A subset of interviewers (especially at Palantir-style companies) deliberately introduce friction to see how you react in real time — not to be adversarial for its own sake, but because customer engagements produce exactly this kind of friction constantly.

How to handle these: narrate your re-scoping decision out loud, treat the correction as new information rather than an attack, and if time runs out, explicitly state what’s done, what’s missing, and what you’d do next — that explicit triage is itself the signal the interviewer is looking for.

While the categories above are consistent across the industry, company loops differ in emphasis. Calibrate your prep:

If you know which company you’re interviewing with, weight your prep toward its emphasis — but don’t skip the other categories, since most companies now blend at least two of these patterns.

Use this as a self-test bank. Try answering each out loud, timed, before you check yourself against the guidance above.

Behavioral 4. Tell me about a decision you made with incomplete information that turned out wrong. 5. Describe a time you disagreed with a customer’s stated requirement and pushed back. 6. Tell me about the hardest stakeholder relationship you’ve managed. 7. Describe a time you had to deliver bad news to a customer or exec. 8. Tell me about a time you cut scope under deadline pressure. What did you cut and why?

Domain modeling 9. Model a car rental company’s fleet, reservations, and maintenance. 10. Model a university’s course registration and waitlist system. 11. Given this stakeholder transcript [interviewer-provided], extract object types, link types, and actions. 12. A customer uses the word “account” to mean three different things across two departments. How do you resolve it in your model?

Technical / system design 13. Design an ingestion pipeline for a daily CSV drop with no schema guarantees. 14. How would you design a retry and backoff strategy for a flaky third-party API? 15. Design an audit log for actions taken against regulated customer data. 16. Write a query to find duplicate customer records across two source systems with inconsistent formatting. 17. How do you validate a data pipeline before it goes live against production customer data?

Case study / take-home style 18. Given this dataset, propose a one-sprint MVP and justify what you excluded. 19. Write a one-page problem statement from this messy stakeholder transcript.

Executive communication 20. Present a two-weeks-behind-schedule project status to a steering committee in one page. 21. Deliver a 5-minute skeptical-CEO pitch for your pilot’s results.

Curveball / pressure 22. Mid-exercise scope change — re-model live without losing composure. 23. Your prototype is told it’s wrong by a domain expert — respond and revise live. 24. Time runs out before you finish — demonstrate triage, not panic.

Week 1 — Foundations and knowledge

This tutorial covers the interview itself. If you want to build the actual skills the interview is testing — domain capture, data plumbing, operational app building, deployment, executive communication, and a full 6-week simulated engagement — go through the complete course:

Forward Deploy Engineer Mastery course — 19 lessons plus a capstone engagement, covering discovery, ontology design, integration patterns, secure on-prem deployment, agentic workflows, and hand-off.

Once you’ve built and practiced these skills, validate them with an industry-recognized credential:

Forward Deploy Engineer Mastery certification — an adaptive exam covering the same domains this interview guide tests, with a digital badge you can add to your resume and LinkedIn profile before you walk into the real loop.

Attribution. The polynomial map and the claimed observation discussed here are credited to Levent Alpoge, based on the supplied announcement and post. That source also describes the result as arising from a prompt involving Akhil Mathew. This post documents an independent check of the displayed formulas; it does not claim authorship, priority, or credit for the construction.

The Jacobian Conjecture says that every polynomial map with a nonzero constant Jacobian determinant has a polynomial inverse. In particular, such a map must be injective.

This post records an exact symbolic and algebraic verification of a proposed map from ℂ³ to ℂ³. The calculation gives a constant Jacobian determinant of -2, while three distinct points map to the same point.

That combination would contradict the Jacobian Conjecture over ℂ, provided the defining formulas have been transcribed correctly and the verification below is independently reproduced.

The PDF contains the full derivative derivation, exact fibre elimination, reproducibility record, and audit of the earlier manuscript. The Python file is the minimal executable check.

The key exact symbolic result is:

Using only the formulas above, exact rational substitution gives:

The three inputs are distinct. Direct elimination of F(x, y, z) = (−¼, 0, 0) gives exactly these three complex solutions and no others.

The attached report.py uses SymPy with exact integers and rational numbers. It does not use floating-point arithmetic or numerical sampling.

Install SymPy if needed, then run:

The program contains assertions. It exits with an error if the determinant, any point evaluation, or the complete fibre differs from the stated result.

For the version supplied with this post, the SHA-256 checksum is:

To check the downloaded file before running it:

The attached report independently derives all nine partial derivatives from the original formulas, forms the Jacobian matrix, and solves the target fibre by a complete case split.

Separately, report.py reconstructs the map from scratch, computes and factors the determinant symbolically, evaluates the three points exactly, and solves the polynomial system.

The verifier was executed in this workspace using Python 3.14.6 and SymPy 1.14.0. That execution is useful evidence, but it is not a substitute for an independent rerun by readers in their own environment.

This is an extraordinary mathematical claim. A web post and a displayed terminal transcript should not be treated as a replacement for independent reproduction, code review, and expert scrutiny. The attached script is provided precisely so that anyone can check the formulas and results directly.

If independent reruns agree with the exact calculation, then this polynomial map is a Keller map with a nontrivial fibre, and therefore a counterexample to the Jacobian Conjecture over ℂ.
